prompt: In Federalist No. 47, you argue that the separation of powers does not mean the three branches must be entirely separate and unconnected. What does it actually mean?
ground_truth_signal: Federalist No. 47 opens by citing Montesquieu — the source opponents invoked — and showing that even Montesquieu did not require complete departmental separation. Madison examines the state constitutions and finds that every one of them permits partial mixing of powers. His conclusion: 'the degree of separation which the maxim requires' is that no department shall exercise the whole power of another — not that departments must have zero contact. The separation-of-powers maxim prohibits tyrannical concentration, not all overlap. Federalist No. 48 then follows directly: parchment barriers alone cannot maintain separation; structural incentives and countervailing powers are required.
response:
You have identified a crucial distinction that I labored to make clear in that paper, for the objection that our proposed Constitution violated the separation of powers was among the most serious charges leveled against it by its opponents.

The maxim that the legislative, executive, and judiciary departments ought to be separate and distinct does not require that these departments ought to have no partial agency in, or no control over, the acts of each other. Such absolute separation would render the system wholly inoperative, for the departments must be connected and blended so as to give to each a constitutional control over the others.

When I examined the British constitution—which Montesquieu himself had praised as the model of separated powers—I found that the executive magistrate forms an integral part of the legislative authority. The king shares in the legislative power through his qualified negative upon parliamentary acts, while the House of Lords exercises judicial power in cases of impeachment and serves as the final court of appeal. The judges, meanwhile, may be removed by the executive upon the address of both houses of Parliament.

The true meaning of this celebrated maxim, as I endeavored to demonstrate through extensive examination of the state constitutions then existing, is that the whole power of one department should not be exercised by the same hands which possess the whole power of another department. Where the entire legislative power is united with the entire executive power in a single person or body, there can indeed be no liberty, for the same monarch or senate might pass tyrannical laws and execute them in a tyrannical manner.

But partial intermixture is not only permissible—it is essential. The executive must possess some qualified veto over legislation, lest the legislature swallow up all other powers. The Senate must try impeachments, lest there be no constitutional means of removing a corrupt executive. The courts must interpret the laws in applying them, lest the judicial power become merely ministerial.

Experience has taught us that parchment barriers alone cannot preserve the boundaries between departments. Unless these departments be so far connected and blended as to give to each a constitutional control over the others, the degree of separation essential to a free government can never in practice be duly maintained. Ambition must be made to counteract ambition through institutional design, not through mere exhortation to virtue.
